summary:
The Rigveda is the oldest of the four Vedas, the sacred texts that form the foundation of Hinduism and of the broader [[event:59|Vedic period]] in the Indian subcontinent. It is a collection of 1,028 hymns composed in Vedic Sanskrit and organized into ten books called mandalas. Scholars generally date its composition to roughly 1500–1200 BC, though some portions may be older, and place it geographically in the region of the Punjab and the upper Indus and Sarasvati river valleys. The hymns were composed by hereditary priestly families and transmitted orally with extraordinary accuracy across centuries before being committed to writing much later.

The hymns of the Rigveda are addressed to a pantheon of deities, most prominently Indra the storm god, Agni the fire deity, Varuna the god of cosmic order, and Soma the deity associated with a ritual drink. They were composed for use in elaborate ritual sacrifices and are the primary source for understanding the religion, cosmology, and social structure of the early Vedic people. The Rigveda also contains speculative hymns on cosmogony and the nature of reality that represent the earliest Sanskrit philosophical inquiry.

The Rigveda is unique in the historical record as the best-preserved text from the Indo-European language family's early history and provides crucial evidence for comparative linguistics, religious history, and the reconstruction of Proto-Indo-European culture. Its transmission through oral tradition, maintained with meticulous attention to pronunciation and meter by Brahmin families across more than three millennia, is itself one of the most remarkable achievements of human memory and cultural transmission. The Rigveda was inscribed in the UNESCO Memory of the World Register in 2007.
